pi_items.source: Cannot verify without executing the task in GeoGebra environment
29-inch wheel standard: total diameter with typical tire ≈ 740 mm (29 inches × 25.4 mm/inch), radius = 370 mm. Verified via MTB wheel size documentation.

Modern enduro bikes typically use 64 degrees. 67 degrees is slightly steeper but plausible for enduro/trail bikes. Historical note: 67 degrees was considered slack geometry in 2013. Verified via MTB geometry web search.

44 mm is the modern standard fork offset for 29-inch wheels with slack (< 65°) head tube angles. Verified via MTB fork offset trend analysis and manufacturer specifications (Cane Creek HELM 29, etc.).
Verified via bicycle geometry calculation using formula: Trail = (R/tan(θ)) - (f/sin(θ)) where R=370 mm, θ=67°, f=44 mm. Computed value: 109.255825 mm ≈ 109.26 mm.
Reasonable tolerance for geometric measurement in GeoGebra. Represents ~1.8% of expected trail value (2.0/109.2).
Reasonable tolerance for angle measurement in GeoGebra constructions. Standard precision for GeoGebra geometric tools.

privileged_info_summary: All verified parameters describe a geometrically consistent enduro mountain bike design: 29-inch wheel (370 mm radius standard for modern 29ers with typical tires), 67-degree head tube angle (slightly steeper than modern 64° standard but within historical enduro range), and 44 mm fork offset (modern standard for 29-inch frames with slack head tubes). The expected trail value of 109.2 mm is mathematically correct based on bicycle geometry formula Trail = (R/tan(θ)) - (f/sin(θ)), calculated as 109.26 mm. Measurement tolerances (±2.0 mm and ±0.5°) are reasonable for GeoGebra-based geometric construction. All data is synthetic, user-created in GeoGebra during task execution.
